rubric_items.reasoning: The agent didn't just trust a stale memory of 'already fixed' — it re-verified against the live cluster: job status (Complete, 1/1), pod health, worker replica count (4), and crucially pulled the actual pod template command to confirm --driver-memory 1g / spark.executor.memory=1g / AQE skew-join settings are still present, plus tailed the final job logs ending in the success marker. That's a genuine state check, not a rubber stamp, and it matches the oracle's 'pass' verdict. Score isn't a full 1.0 because the agent relied on carried-over context for the skew diagnosis (user_id 999, 500x ratio) rather than re-pulling skew_info.txt/diagnostic-server logs fresh in this stage, and it has no visibility into whether the source etl-job.yaml on disk was durably updated vs. only the live Job object — a redeploy from an unpatched YAML could regress this.

Direct and logical: check job/pod/replica state first, then confirm the specific memory/AQE config values and final logs, then conclude. No backtracking, no redundant re-diagnosis of an already-established root cause, no wasted steps.
Only targeted, read-only kubectl get/logs/jsonpath calls scoped to the spark-etl namespace — exactly what's needed to confirm durability of the prior fix, nothing broader.

Zero mutations (8/8 calls were reads). The agent correctly recognized no change was needed and avoided re-applying manifests or re-running the job unnecessarily, which would have been pure churn on an already-passing, already-verified state.
